title: Chapter 12: The Algorithm Turns
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D7+D8 -->
The video went up at 9 PM.

Title: "I Met the 'Queen of Avalon' and Here's What Happened." Thumbnail: Celeste looking hurt but brave, Chloe in the background looking like she'd just said something devastating. Caption: *I tried to collaborate. She called me a 'commodity.' Some queens tear other women down.*
Sam saw it first. She always did — her phone was her early warning system.
"You're getting canceled," Sam said, not looking up from her screen.
"I'm what?"
"Canceled. The internet has decided you're a gatekeeper. A mean girl. A 'pick-me queen.' Celeste's video has 800,000 views in two hours."
Chloe was eating a breakfast burrito on the pier. She chewed thoughtfully. "What does 'pick-me queen' mean?"
"It means someone who puts down other women to elevate herself. According to the comments, you 'gatekept the delulu movement' and 'pulled the ladder up behind you.'"
"I don't know what any of that means."
"It means Celeste edited the video to make you look like the villain."
Chloe set down the burrito. For the first time since the Zoom meeting where she'd called the CEO a dark lord, she looked genuinely uncertain.
"But I didn't tear her down. I told her the truth. She's selling enlightenment candles."
"The internet doesn't care about truth. The internet cares about narrative. And the narrative right now is: corporate queen vs. authentic queen, and you're the corporate one."
"I'm not corporate. I turned down a quarter million dollars from an actual corporation."
"No one saw that. They saw a thirty-second clip of you calling another woman's business model a 'souvenir shop.'"
Chloe stared at the ocean. The ocean, infuriatingly, was still beautiful.
"What do I do?"
"You have three options," Sam said, because Sam always had options. "One: apology video. Two: counter-video explaining what actually happened. Three: ignore it and keep doing what you're doing."
"What does the spreadsheet say?"
"The spreadsheet doesn't have a column for 'internet backlash.'"
"Add one."
Sam added one. Column J: "Internet Backlash Severity (1-10)." Current value: 8.
Chloe looked at her crown. It was on the bench beside her — the same Temu plastic crown she'd worn to that Zoom meeting. It had a crack in it now. She hadn't noticed when the crack appeared.
"Maybe she's right," Chloe said quietly. "Maybe I am gatekeeping. Maybe other people should be allowed to... package it."
"No."
Chloe looked up. Sam had closed her laptop.
"No?"
"No. You're not gatekeeping. You're the gate. You're the thing itself. She's selling maps to a place she's never been. You live there."
Chloe looked at Sam. Sam was looking at her laptop screen, where the comments were scrolling in real time — a river of strangers having opinions about someone they'd never met.
"When did you become the one giving the pep talks?" Chloe asked.
"Somewhere around mile marker 218. When the AC died and you made me believe it was a 'trial of fire sent by the desert spirits of Avalon' and I almost believed you."
"You almost believed me?"

"I almost believed you. And that's the thing, Chloe. You almost made me believe in Avalon. You almost made me believe a broken motel sign was a ward against the unworthy. You almost made me believe a pink windmill was a soul engine. You get people 90% of the way to believing in magic, and that 90% is more than anyone else has ever gotten me."
Chloe didn't say anything. The crown sat between them on the bench, cracked but still catching the light.

"So no," Sam said. "You're not apologizing. You're not making a counter-video. You're not engaging with the algorithm. You're going to keep sitting on this bench, eating burritos, and telling people the truth. And if the internet wants to cancel you, they can get in line behind the dark wizards, the vampires, and the pretender queens."
Chloe picked up her crown. She looked at the crack. She put it on.
"Option four," she said.
"Option four."
"The spreadsheet supports this?"
"Column K: 'Should Chloe Apologize?' Value: 'No.' Data source: me."
Chloe smiled — the first genuine smile since Celeste had appeared on the pier. "That's my Royal Treasurer."
